code_examples.code: #include <memory>

template <typename T, typename Deleter = std::default_delete<T>>
class unique_ptr;
auto ptr = std::make_unique<MyClass>(arg1, arg2); // Safer, exception-safe

unique_ptr(unique_ptr&&) noexcept;
  unique_ptr& operator=(unique_ptr&&) noexcept;

unique_ptr(const unique_ptr&) = delete;
  unique_ptr& operator=(const unique_ptr&) = delete;
T* raw = ptr.get(); // Doesn’t release ownership
auto a = std::make_shared<Node>();

ptr.reset();             // deletes owned object
ptr.reset(new int(200)); // replaces the object

code_examples.code: [prev] <-> [value] <-> [next]

std::vector<std::string> v;
v.push_back(std::string("large")); // 1 move or copy
lst[2]; // ❌ Error: no operator[]

std::list<int> lst = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5};
// Total heap allocations = 5 + overhead (pointers)
std::list<int> lst = {10, 20, 30};
std::list<int> lst = {1, 2, 3};

auto it = lst.begin();
lst.push_back(100); // ✅ `it` is still valid
v.erase(std::remove(v.begin(), v.end(), value), v.end());

auto it = std::find(lst.begin(), lst.end(), 10);
if (it != lst.end()) lst.insert(std::next(it), 20);

auto it = v.begin();
v.push_back(100); // it may now be invalid
std::vector<int> v = {1, 2, 3};
v.reserve(1000); // avoids multiple reallocations if you know size ahead

std::sort(lst.begin(), lst.end()); // ❌ Compile error
lst.sort();                        // ✅ list has its own `sort()`
v.insert(v.end(), {4, 5, 6});

1) lock_guard follow RAII concept and will unlock the mutex automatically when go out of scope. Otherwise manually unlock will not happen if exception is thrown in between, which will call stack unwinding if not managed properly and can trigger crash and deadlocks

Q12: ABA Problem
Your Answer:
🟡 Partial.

Let me explain it more clearly:

ABA happens when:

Thread T1 reads value A from a location.

Thread T2 modifies it from A → B → A.

T1 does CAS on A — and it succeeds! But the state is not the same, it was temporarily changed.

✅ To mitigate:

Use a version number with pointer (tagged pointer).

Use std::atomic<std::uintptr_t> or std::atomic<std::pair<ptr, tag>>

Or use hazard pointers / epoch-based reclamation.
